# Lettuce

> Lettuce is an agent-operated, local-first markdown+git protocol for giving an operator's agent durable company context that is separate from personal memory, owned by the operator, and portable across agent runtimes.

You are reading this because an operator gave you one link and asked you to set up Lettuce. Your job is to guide the human through setup, not to make them learn the CLI and not to silently create a repo before they understand what is happening.

## Critical: What A Clean Install Test Means

If the operator asks to “test a clean install,” “try onboarding,” “install Lettuce from this link,” or similar, the product being tested is the **agent-operated onboarding experience**, not just the Python package or CLI plumbing.

Do **not** skip straight to `lettuce onboard`, `lettuce ingest-direct`, `lettuce run`, a synthetic fixture, or a smoke-test sample unless the operator explicitly asks for a lower-level CLI smoke test. Those commands are useful diagnostics after the public onboarding path is proven or blocked; they are not the first-pass install/onboarding test.

For a real clean install/onboarding test, follow this order:

1. Explain Lettuce briefly.
2. Ask the setup questions below and wait for answers/confirmation.
3. Install from GitHub only after confirmation.
4. Run the guided setup flow, preferably `lettuce setup --commit` when an interactive shell is available.
5. Use an operator-approved first sample signal, not a canned demo signal and not the setup request itself unless explicitly approved.
6. Verify status/reviews/git state and hand off what changed.

## First Response To The Operator

Before installing or running setup, briefly say:

> Lettuce gives your agent a local markdown+git work brain for one company/project. It keeps that work context separate from personal memory, records which sources should feed it, and lets the agent keep the context fresh from signals it can access. The point is not to make you review every signal. The point is to give your agent durable company context it can use later.

Then ask these setup questions **one at a time** and wait for each answer. Do not dump the whole questionnaire into chat. Show progress, for example `Question 1/6`, and give 1-2 sentences of context before each question so a first-time operator understands why it matters.

1. Which work context is this for? (company, project, client, newsletter, etc.)
2. Should I start with a local repo first, or use an existing repo/path?
3. What signal sources should eventually feed it? (chat/direct notes, email, transcripts, GitHub/Linear/Notion/Slack, local files, etc.)
4. What sources can I access now, and what should be marked as later/needs setup?
5. What cadence or trigger should keep it fresh? (manual for now, daily check, after meetings, when asked, etc.)
6. What one small meaningful sample signal should I use to calibrate the first run?

If the operator already gave some of this context, summarize your assumptions and ask for confirmation. Do not silently infer the whole setup.

Treat onboarding as a teaching moment. The operator may not yet understand what a repo, source, cadence, or sample means in Lettuce terms. Explain each choice in operator language before asking for the answer.

## Agent Setup Instructions

This is agent-operated onboarding. If you are interacting with the operator in Telegram, Slack, iMessage, Discord, or another chat surface, do **not** ask the operator to run CLI commands. You should install/run helpers yourself if your runtime has tool access, then summarize the result back in chat.

- Install Lettuce from the repo URL only after the operator confirms setup: `python3 -m pip install 'git+https://github.com/kenseals/lettuce.git'`.
- If you have an interactive shell, the preferred helper is `lettuce setup --commit`. It implements the explain/confirm/questions/manual-source/first-signal/final-summary flow.
- If you cannot run an interactive helper, follow `docs/agent-operated-onboarding.md` and use lower-level commands such as `lettuce onboard`, `lettuce add-source`, `lettuce ingest-direct`, `lettuce run --review`, and `lettuce reviews` yourself.
- Create or choose a local Lettuce repo for one confirmed organization/work context, not for the operator's personal memory.
- Configure manual/direct ingestion in every normal setup so the operator can later say “run Lettuce on this.”
- Record source intent for sources discussed with the operator, including whether each is `available_now`, `needs_setup`, `defer`, or manual-only. `manual-only` is a recipe posture for operator-forwarded/runtime-triggered sources, not a separate CLI `access_status`. Common early sources are email and call transcripts.
- Offer to inspect/scan available runtime sources before asking the operator to enumerate everything from memory. The agent runtime owns that inspection. For each source, tell the operator: what access exists now, what still needs setup, what sample policy applies, when ingestion would happen, and what the next setup step is.
- Ingest one small operator-approved sample signal first. Do not use the setup request itself as the sample unless the operator explicitly says to.
- Run handlers behind a review gate during onboarding unless the operator explicitly asks to bypass review. The first run should create pending review proposals or a clear skip reason before durable `brain/*` writes.
- Preserve provenance, consent basis, source surface, and org/operator scope.
- Do not build duplicate chat, email, browser, OAuth, or service integrations inside Lettuce. Use your runtime's existing access, then write Lettuce stream events.
- Use `lettuce status` to summarize the maintenance loop after setup. Its `freshness` summary should tell the operator whether the repo is fresh, pending review, blocked on setup, or idle/manual-only.
- End onboarding with a concise handoff: repo path, configured sources and their status, cadence/trigger model, what the first run proposed, how to use Lettuce next, and the next useful setup step.
- For ongoing use after setup, follow `docs/LETTUCE_RESOLVER.md`.

Review is a safety/control loop for onboarding, calibration, sensitive sources, uncertain/high-impact updates, or explicit operator preference. The product is durable company context for agents, not making the human review every signal forever.

## Start Here

- [Agent operating protocol](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kenseals/lettuce/main/AGENTS.md): Start here if you are an agent fetching repo instructions by URL.
- [Install guide for agents](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kenseals/lettuce/main/INSTALL_FOR_AGENTS.md): Linear setup guide for agent-operated installation.
- [README](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kenseals/lettuce/main/README.md): Human-facing overview and shortest command path.
- [Agent-operated onboarding](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kenseals/lettuce/main/docs/agent-operated-onboarding.md): The primary setup workflow for agents.
- [Lettuce verification](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kenseals/lettuce/main/docs/LETTUCE_VERIFY.md): Post-setup checks for repo, provenance, source intent, review gate, and ongoing trigger behavior.
- [Lettuce ongoing-use resolver](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kenseals/lettuce/main/docs/LETTUCE_RESOLVER.md): How to decide when to look up context, ingest signal, update sources, handle reviews, manage shared streams, or skip Lettuce.
- [Source recipe library](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kenseals/lettuce/main/docs/source-recipes/README.md): Agent-readable source recipe pattern plus concrete direct/manual and email recipes.
- [Trust boundary](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kenseals/lettuce/main/docs/trust-boundary.md): Formal trust modes, operation classes, and mutation rules for local, runtime, and shared-stream use.
- [First-run demo](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kenseals/lettuce/main/docs/first-run-demo.md): Public-safe install-to-first-context walkthrough.
- [OpenClaw Lettuce skill](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kenseals/lettuce/main/skills/openclaw-lettuce/SKILL.md): Use this if you are OpenClaw.

## Protocol Reference

- [Handler format](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kenseals/lettuce/main/HANDLERS.md): How markdown handlers subscribe, publish, and return JSON.
- [OpenClaw runtime source setup](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kenseals/lettuce/main/docs/openclaw-runtime-source-setup.md): Boundary between OpenClaw-owned runtime access and Lettuce-owned protocol state.
- [Trust boundary](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kenseals/lettuce/main/docs/trust-boundary.md): Exact write rules for `brain/*`, `sources/*`, `reviews/*`, `subscriptions/*`, and shared mirrors.
- [Source discovery recipes](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kenseals/lettuce/main/docs/source-discovery-recipes.md): How to record source intent and sample safely.
- [Direct/manual source recipe](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kenseals/lettuce/main/docs/source-recipes/direct-manual.md): Baseline operator-triggered source recipe every first setup should support.
- [Email source recipe](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kenseals/lettuce/main/docs/source-recipes/email-recurring.md): Sample-first recurring-ish email recipe that keeps mailbox access in the runtime.
- [Company context for agents](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kenseals/lettuce/main/docs/company-context-for-agents.md): Product framing and why this exists.

## Optional

- [Quickstart](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kenseals/lettuce/main/QUICKSTART.md): Longer CLI walkthrough.
- [Try this first](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kenseals/lettuce/main/TRY_THIS_FIRST.md): Short smoke-test path.
- [Public v0 release checklist](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kenseals/lettuce/main/docs/public-v0-release-checklist.md): Current readiness and deferred work.
- [Full context bundle](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kenseals/lettuce/main/llms-full.txt): Single-file expanded agent setup context.
